front: Work: contains the ontological argument, which begins with the definition of God as 'that which none greater can be imagined' and argues such a being must exist
Philosopher: Gaunilo of Marmoutiers responded to his ontological argument by postulating a 'Lost Island' in a critique titled In Behalf of the Fool
Philosopher: wrote the Proslogion to persuade the 'Fool of the Psalms'
Philosopher: Descartes's variation on his most famous argument makes up most of the fifth Meditation on First Philosophy
Philosopher: in a 1970 article titled for this thinker, David Lewis argued that the ontological argument is invalid because actuality is indexical
Philosopher: Alvin Plantinga developed a 'victorious' modal version of his most famous argument in The Nature of Necessity using axiom S5, building on Hartshorne and Malcolm
Work: opens with the invocation 'come now, insignificant man, fly for a moment from your affairs'
Philosopher: used the example of a painter who conceives of his painting before executing it to support his ontological argument
Philosopher: Duns Scotus's arguments for the univocity of being were based on this thinker's arguments
Concept: Norman Malcolm argued that Chapter Three of the Proslogion constitutes a second, distinct version of this argument from Chapter Two
Concept: Immanuel Kant objected to the ontological argument by arguing that this property is 'not a predicate'
Concept: Kurt Gödel's modal version of the ontological argument was criticized by Jordan Sobel for leading to this problem
Philosopher: Charles Hartshorne used modal logic and dipolar attributes to update his ontological argument in The Logic of Perfection
Philosopher: Alexius Meinong developed a variant of his ontological argument based on a unique theory of objects
Concept: Graham Oppy's namesake text divides this argument into six subtypes
Concept: Robert Maydole developed the MPA and 'temporal-contingency' arguments supporting this idea
Philosopher: Iris Murdoch's The Sovereignty of the Good includes a version of his ontological argument
Work: framed as a dialogue with student Boso, argues that Christ's crucifixion was necessary to bring 'satisfaction' to God rather than serving as a 'ransom'
work: Satisfaction Theory of Atonement (Cur Deus Homo)

front: Philosopher: Peter Abelard opposed this thinker's satisfaction theory, claiming Christ's death was to exemplify perfect love
work: Satisfaction Theory of Atonement (Cur Deus Homo)

front: Philosopher: his satisfaction theory of atonement replaced the earlier ransom theory as the dominant account in Catholicism
work: Satisfaction Theory of Atonement (Cur Deus Homo)

front: Philosopher: his motto was 'faith seeking understanding' and he famously wrote 'I believe so that I may understand'
Philosopher: R.W. Southern wrote two biographies of this thinker and argued in The Making of the Middle Ages that he originated a genre of 'ardent and effusive self-disclosure'
Philosopher: at the Council of Bari, compared the Father-Son relationship to man's relationship to himself through memory, published in On the Procession of the Holy Spirit
Philosopher: also wrote the Monologion in addition to his more famous Proslogion
Philosopher: used the example of 'white' signifying whiteness vs. a white thing to illustrate per se and per aliud forms of signification
Philosopher: considered the 'father of Scholasticism'
Philosopher: his On Truth employed contrasting student and teacher characters, exemplifying the Latin dialogue form
Philosopher: claimed that 'the eye of a needle' referred to a small pedestrian gate in the walls of Jerusalem
